title: Boiled bacon and cabbage
subtitle: Gammon joint simmered with aromatics, with braised cabbage and parsley sauce
excerpt: A gammon joint simmered slowly in water with bay, peppercorns, and aromatics until tender, then served with briefly braised cabbage and a sharp parsley sauce. The plainest and most satisfying of Irish and British boiled dinners.
sourceNotes: Boiled bacon and cabbage is a central dish in Irish domestic cooking, documented in Maura Laverty, Feasting Galore (1961), and Myrtle Allen, The Ballymaloe Cookbook (1977). Florence Irwin, Irish Country Recipes (1937), also records the dish as a standard of Irish farmhouse cooking. The combination of boiled cured pork and cabbage is one of the oldest continuous traditions in both Irish and British cooking.

content.text: Boiled bacon and cabbage is one of those dishes whose simplicity is its greatest virtue. The bacon joint does all the work: simmered slowly in water with a few aromatics, it produces a quiet, porky broth and meat that is tender throughout. The cabbage is cooked briefly in the same liquid so it takes on a little of the flavour. The parsley sauce is sharp and bright against the salt of the pork.

Use a gammon joint rather than a smoked bacon joint: the smoked version can dominate. If the gammon has been heavily cured, soak it in cold water for several hours before cooking to reduce the salt. Taste the cooking liquor before adding the cabbage: if it is very salty, cook the cabbage in a separate pot of fresh water.

items.name: Savoy cabbage
items.prepNote: outer leaves removed, cut into wedges

content.text: Place {{gammon-joint}} of gammon in a large pot and cover with cold water. Bring to the boil, pour off the water, and rinse the pot. This removes the initial grey foam. Cover with fresh cold water and bring back to a gentle simmer. Add the halved onion, {{carrot}} roughly chopped carrot, {{celery}} celery sticks, bay leaves, and peppercorns. Simmer gently, partially covered, for 20 minutes per 500 g of joint, plus 20 minutes, so approximately 1 hour 40 minutes for a 1.2 kg joint.
Cook the cabbage and make the sauce

For the last 15 minutes, add {{cabbage-savoy}} cabbage wedges to the cooking liquor and simmer until tender. Meanwhile, melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a saucepan over medium heat. Add {{plain-flour}} of flour and stir for 2 minutes. Gradually whisk in {{whole-milk}} of milk until smooth. Cook, stirring, until the sauce thickens. Season with pepper. Stir in {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley just before serving, parsley added too early loses its colour and brightness.
Lift the gammon from the pot and rest for 10 minutes. Carve into thick slices. Serve with the cabbage wedges and a generous pour of parsley sauce. Mashed potato alongside absorbs the sauce well.

items.fix: Next time, soak the joint in cold water overnight in the fridge. Change the water once. The first boil and discard of water also helps significantly.
items.cause: The joint was heavily cured and not soaked

items.fix: Whisk vigorously, lumps in white sauce can usually be beaten out over heat. If not, pass through a sieve.
items.cause: Milk was added too quickly or not whisked constantly

items.fix: Add the cabbage wedges for no more than 15 minutes. They should be tender but still hold shape.
Gammon is forgiving, but very long cooking over high heat will dry it out. Calculate by weight and use a gentle simmer throughout.
items.cause: Cooked for too long or at too high a heat

content.text: Boiled bacon and cabbage is rooted in Irish farmhouse cooking. Cured pork was the steady preserved meat. Cabbage held through most of the year. In its plainest form, the dish asks for a pot of water, a few aromatics, and time. The parsley sauce is the one trim that lifts it. Its fresh, sharp note is just what the salt pork needs.
